target.title: fix(status): keep default JSON scan lean
target.body:
## Summary
- keep default `status --json` on the lean status path by skipping update checks, task registry summaries, memory inspection, and local status-RPC fallback work unless `--all` is requested
- cap the default fast JSON gateway probe to a short presence probe
- preserve the deeper diagnostics behind `status --json --all`
- harden local status-RPC fallback when a failed probe omits `auth`

## Why
`openclaw status --json --timeout 3000` could take tens of seconds on Pi-class hosts because the default JSON path still triggered heavyweight diagnostic work. In PD One/Joi testing, the gateway itself was healthy and `/health` was fast; the CLI status path was the slow, misleading part.

The default JSON command should be safe as a health probe. Heavier diagnostics belong behind `--all`.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Default `openclaw status --json --timeout 3000` was too slow for health probing on Pi-class hosts because it still performed heavyweight local diagnostics even when the gateway was healthy.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on a Raspberry Pi-class Linux host, using built `dist/index.js` with an isolated `OPENCLAW_HOME` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  pnpm build
  OPENCLAW_HOME="$TMP" OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$TMP/openclaw.json" node dist/index.js status --json --timeout 3000
  OPENCLAW_HOME="$TMP" OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$TMP/openclaw.json" node dist/index.js status --json --timeout 3000 --all
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the real local OpenClaw run:
  ```text
  ARGS status --json --timeout 3000
  rc=0 elapsed_ms=4966 out=4097 err=0
  {
    "runtimeVersion": "2026.5.5",
    "tasks": 0,
    "channelSummary": 0,
    "memory": null,
    "gatewayReachable": false
  }

  ARGS status --json --timeout 3000 --all
  rc=0 elapsed_ms=5994 out=6539 err=116
  gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: unauthorized: gateway token missing (provide gateway auth token)
  {
    "runtimeVersion": "2026.5.5",
    "tasks": 0,
    "channelSummary": 0,
    "memory": null,
    "gatewayReachable": false
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** Default JSON status completed successfully with no stderr and without task/channel/memory summaries. The deeper diagnostic path remains available behind `--all` and still emits the heavier gateway diagnostic behavior.
- **What was not tested:** I did not publish this branch to the PD One production runtime; the production observation was used to identify the slow status path, and this proof uses a built local OpenClaw checkout.

The original production symptom on PD One/Joi was much worse: `status --json --timeout 3000` could take roughly 38–71s while `/health` stayed fast, showing the status path was the slow diagnostic layer rather than a dead gateway.

## Verification
- `pnpm test src/commands/status.scan.fast-json.test.ts src/commands/status.scan.shared.test.ts src/commands/status.summary.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/commands/status.scan-execute.ts src/commands/status.scan-overview.ts src/commands/status.scan.bootstrap-shared.ts src/commands/status.scan.fast-json.ts src/commands/status.scan.shared.ts src/commands/status.summary.ts src/commands/status.scan.fast-json.test.ts src/commands/status.scan.shared.test.ts src/commands/status.summary.test.ts` ✅
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/commands/status.scan-execute.ts src/commands/status.scan-overview.ts src/commands/status.scan.bootstrap-shared.ts src/commands/status.scan.fast-json.ts src/commands/status.scan.shared.ts src/commands/status.summary.ts src/commands/status.scan.fast-json.test.ts src/commands/status.scan.shared.test.ts src/commands/status.summary.test.ts` ✅
- `pnpm build` ✅

Note: an earlier local `pnpm check:changed` run passed conflict/changelog/duplicate coverage/core typecheck lanes, then hit the 600s tool timeout during broad `lint:core`. After refreshing dependencies with `pnpm install`, targeted lint and build passed cleanly.

target.title: feat(browser): add browserbase profile driver with per-attach connectUrl resolution
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a new `browserbase` profile driver to the OpenClaw browser plugin. Profiles using this driver declare a Browserbase keep-alive session id and an env var name for the API key; on every CDP attach OpenClaw fetches a fresh `connectUrl` from `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` and hands it to the existing CDP pipeline. Eliminates the stale-signing-key fragility that breaks stateful Browserbase work today.

```jsonc
"browserbase-lexzur": {
  "driver": "browserbase",
  "browserbaseSessionId": "44589bac-33f0-4080-9eff-f1b3e3a0bf9c",
  "browserbaseApiKeyEnv": "BROWSERBASE_API_KEY",
  "attachOnly": true
}
```

## Why

The existing `cdpUrl: "wss://...?signingKey=..."` model puts a short-lived JWT into static config. Browserbase rotates the signing key well before the session itself expires — verified empirically with two consecutive `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` calls returning different `signingKey` values for the same session. Once the embedded key ages out, every CDP attach gets `1008 unauthorized` and any multi-step stateful workflow (login → multi-step → submit) breaks mid-flow.

The fix is the documented Browserbase pattern: fetch a fresh `connectUrl` via the Sessions API on each attach. Bearer auth comes from the operator's own credentials, refreshed on each request, no embedded lifetime to track.

## What changed

### New module — `extensions/browser/src/browser/browserbase-session.ts`

- `fetchBrowserbaseConnectUrl(sessionId, apiKeyEnv, opts)` — issues `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` with `X-BB-API-Key`. 8s default timeout, combines internal AbortController with caller-supplied `signal`. Three named error classes: `BrowserbaseAuthConfigError`, `BrowserbaseSessionUnavailableError`, `BrowserbaseSessionMalformedError`.
- `withResolvedCdpUrl(profile, opts)` — identity for non-browserbase drivers (same reference returned, zero allocations, no fetch); shallow-copy with freshly resolved `cdpUrl` / `cdpHost` / `cdpIsLoopback: false` for browserbase drivers.
- **No caching.** Two calls = two HTTP requests by design — the whole point is signing-key rotation.

### Resolver branch — `extensions/browser/src/browser/config.ts`

- `resolveProfile()` gains a `browserbase` branch that validates `browserbaseSessionId` is UUID-shaped and `browserbaseApiKeyEnv` matches `/^[A-Z][A-Z0-9_]*$/`. Returns `cdpUrl: ""` — URL is resolved lazily at attach time.
- Operator-friendly fail-fast at config load (not on first hot-path attach) for missing/malformed fields.

### Capability branch — `extensions/browser/src/browser/profile-capabilities.ts`

- Third branch for `driver === "browserbase"` → `mode: "remote-cdp"`, `isRemote: true`, `usesPersistentPlaywright: true`, all `/json/*` endpoint flags off (Browserbase doesn't expose JSON tab endpoints; everything goes through the signed WS connectUrl).

### Wired into CDP attach call sites

- `server-context.tab-ops.ts` — `readTabs` + `openTab` Playwright legs.
- `server-context.selection.ts` — `focusTab` + `closeTab` Playwright legs.
- `server-context.availability.ts` — `isReachable` / `isHttpReachable` probe via cheap `fetchBrowserbaseConnectUrl` for browserbase profiles (instead of full CDP WS attach per status poll).

### Default-timeout bump (separate commit `700639f1df`)

Based on real-world transcontinental remote-CDP topology (SFO-hosted Browserbase + Linux gateway). Previous 1500/2000 defaults were too aggressive — left zero margin on healthy attaches under transit jitter.

- `remoteCdpTimeoutMs`: 1500 → 3000
- `remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs` floor: `max(remoteCdpTimeoutMs*2, 5000)` → resolves to 6000 by default. Preserves the `handshake ≥ 2× http` invariant for operators who customize one without bumping the other.

### Docs (separate commit `68f68af6f7`)

New subsection in `docs/tools/browser.md` covering keep-alive sessions, auto-create vs session-id distinction, the `POST /v1/sessions` curl example with `keepAlive: true`, the new profile shape, why static-URL signingKey rotation is fragile, and the SecretRef follow-up note.

## Tests

```
extensions/browser/src/browser/browserbase-session.test.ts     13 passed
extensions/browser/src/browser/config.browserbase.test.ts       8 passed
```

Full extension-browser suite: **103 files, 1099 passed, 1 pre-existing skip, 0 failed.**

## Live smoke test

Verified end-to-end against the production Browserbase API with a fresh keepAlive session:

| Test | Result |
|---|---|
| Happy path live `GET /v1/sessions/<id>` | ✅ 584ms |
| No-caching contract (two calls = different signingKeys) | ✅ confirmed signing-key rotation working |
| `BrowserbaseAuthConfigError` raised on missing env var | ✅ |
| `withResolvedCdpUrl` shallow-copy preserves immutability | ✅ |
| Identity passthrough for non-browserbase drivers | ✅ same reference returned |

## Out of scope (deliberately)

- **SecretRef supported-surface expansion** for `browserbaseApiKeyEnv`. The new field is read from `process.env` at attach time, not via SecretRef resolution. Follow-up PR to add the path to the canonical credential surface. Operators who want a 1Password-only secret can inject the env var via `op run --env-file=...` at gateway start.
- **Auto-mint-on-stale-session fallback** — when the configured session id is no longer `RUNNING`, this PR fails loudly with `BrowserbaseSessionUnavailableError`. Auto-minting would mean OpenClaw writes its own config at runtime; that's a separate design conversation.
- **Renaming the legacy `browserbase` auto-create profile** to `browserbase-ephemeral`. Follow-up release with migration tooling.

## Risk + rollback

Risk surface is narrow — the new code path only activates when a profile declares `driver: "browserbase"`. Existing `openclaw` and `existing-session` profiles get an identity passthrough in `withResolvedCdpUrl` (same reference, zero allocations, no fetch). Default-timeout bump is conservative and only affects remote CDP profiles; loopback `openclaw` profiles are unaffected.

Rollback is a revert. Existing deployments without a `browserbase`-driver profile are bit-identical in behavior except for the +1.5s remote-CDP timeout bump.

## Lexzur audit context

Lexzur audit on 2026-05-17 burned 4-5 Browserbase sessions and as many OTPs trying to drive a multi-step SSO ticket form before identifying the static-signingKey-in-URL pattern as the root cause. This PR is the permanent fix for that class of failure — multi-step Browserbase flows can now complete in a single keep-alive session with cookies persisting across tool calls.

target.title: Scope agent prompt surfaces by runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR separates runtime-specific prompt fragments by explicit prompt surface so PI, CLI, ACP, native Codex app-server, and subagent prompts do not inherit each other's assumptions.

The core issue is not that PI's prompt is wrong. PI can keep the full OpenClaw control prompt. The problem is that native Codex already has its own harness prompt stack, so OpenClaw must add only the parts it owns instead of accidentally layering a second PI/Codex policy prompt on top of Codex.

## Why This Update

After the first implementation, I ran a second adversarial audit against the actual Codex native app-server prompt stack. That audit confirmed the surface split is the right abstraction, and it found a few places where the PR description needed to be explicit about double-prompt risks:

- Codex native app-server runs layer OpenClaw `developerInstructions` on top of Codex model/base/personality instructions.
- PI prompt text is valid for PI, but becomes risky when copied into Codex native or CLI prompt lanes.
- Some OpenClaw overrides are intentional and should remain, especially source-channel reply delivery and Codex-vs-OpenClaw delegation routing.
- Some broader OpenClaw prompt/context material should be narrowed or handled as follow-up because it can compete with Codex-native AGENTS, skills, and personality handling.

## Prompt Surface Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Runtime["Selected harness/runtime"] --> Surface["AgentPromptSurfaceKind"]
  Surface --> PI["pi_main"]
  Surface --> CLI["cli_backend"]
  Surface --> ACP["acp_backend"]
  Surface --> Codex["codex_app_server"]
  Surface --> Subagent["subagent"]

  PI --> PIPrompt["Full OpenClaw/PI system prompt"]
  CLI --> CLIPrompt["CLI backend prompt without PI fallback text"]
  ACP --> ACPPrompt["ACP-specific guidance"]
  Codex --> CodexPrompt["Codex developerInstructions only"]
  Subagent --> SubPrompt["Subagent-scoped guidance only"]
```

The boundary this PR introduces is simple: prompt routing follows the selected harness/runtime prompt surface, not provider-string matching and not a single shared global string bucket.

## Native Codex Layering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  CodexModel["Codex model/base instructions"] --> CodexTurn["Codex native turn context"]
  CodexPersonality["Codex personality/model messages"] --> CodexTurn
  CodexApps["Codex apps/skills/plugins instructions"] --> CodexTurn
  CodexAgents["Codex AGENTS.md/project docs as contextual user content"] --> CodexTurn

  OpenClawDev["OpenClaw developerInstructions"] --> CodexTurn
  OpenClawTools["OpenClaw dynamic tools"] --> CodexTurn
  UserPrompt["OpenClaw user/source prompt"] --> CodexTurn

  CodexTurn --> Model["Responses request"]
```

OpenClaw does not replace Codex `baseInstructions` in this PR. It uses Codex's `developerInstructions` lane for OpenClaw-owned behavior. That is the right lane, but it means we have to keep OpenClaw additions narrow.

## What Changes

- Adds a small prompt-surface helper and public plugin command guidance types for scoped agent prompt guidance.
- Keeps legacy `agentPromptGuidance: string[]` behavior while allowing structured `{ text, surfaces }` entries.
- Scopes Codex native command guidance to the main PI/OpenClaw agent prompt surface instead of subagent or CLI prompt lanes.
- Applies the same surface-scoped command guidance when compacted embedded runs rebuild their system prompt.
- Keeps PI's empty-tool fallback intact, while CLI backend prompts no longer mention PI or OpenClaw tool workflow hints when no OpenClaw tools are injected.
- Makes Codex app-server developer reply instructions conditional on `sourceReplyDeliveryMode`, so automatic visible replies are not told to use `message` unconditionally.
- Incorporates adversarial-review hardening: structured prompt-surface validation now follows the file's lazy-init pattern for bundled startup safety, and subagent-scoped guidance renders even when ACP is disabled.

## Scenarios

| Scenario | Before | After / Intended Behavior |
| --- | --- | --- |
| PI has no explicit tool lines | PI fallback text is valid and should remain | PI still renders the OpenClaw tool fallback |
| CLI backend has no OpenClaw tools | CLI could inherit PI/OpenClaw fallback wording | CLI says no OpenClaw tool list is injected and uses only backend tools |
| Subagent prompt is built | Global Codex command guidance could appear in child prompts | Subagent receives only subagent-scoped guidance |
| Native Codex automatic source reply | Codex could be told to use `message` even when Codex delivery is automatic | Automatic mode says to use the active Codex delivery path |
| Native Codex `message_tool_only` source reply | Codex final-answer habit can conflict with OpenClaw channel delivery | OpenClaw intentionally overrides and tells Codex to use `message` |
| Native Codex delegation | Codex native `spawn_agent` and OpenClaw `sessions_spawn` can compete | Codex subagents use native `spawn_agent`; OpenClaw/ACP delegation uses `sessions_spawn` |
| ACP backend prompt | ACP-specific docs/snapshots can become the oracle for Codex app-server behavior | ACP remains its own prompt surface and does not define native Codex behavior |

## Codex Conflict Audit

These are the specific conflict classes checked against the actual Codex native prompt stack:

### 1. Codex base/personality versus OpenClaw GPT-5 overlay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A["Codex base/model/personality prompt"] --> C["Native Codex run"]
  B["OpenClaw GPT-5 behavior/personality overlay"] --> C
  C --> Risk["Risk: duplicate style/execution policy"]
```

Severity: high for native Codex if left broad.

Recommendation: native Codex should let Codex own base/model/personality behavior. OpenClaw should keep only OpenClaw-owned routing, channel delivery, and runtime guidance in `developerInstructions`.

### 2. Visible reply delivery versus Codex final-answer behavior

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant User
  participant OpenClaw
  participant Codex
  participant Channel

  User->>OpenClaw: Source-channel request
  OpenClaw->>Codex: developerInstructions with delivery mode
  alt message_tool_only
    Codex->>OpenClaw: message(action=send)
    OpenClaw->>Channel: visible reply
  else automatic
    Codex->>OpenClaw: normal Codex delivery path
    OpenClaw->>Channel: route once
  end
```

Severity: medium-high, but intentional.

Recommendation: keep the OpenClaw override only for `message_tool_only`. Automatic mode should stay neutral, which this PR now enforces.

### 3. Codex native subagents versus OpenClaw sessions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  Ask["Delegation request"] --> IsCodex{"Codex-native subagent?"}
  IsCodex -->|Yes| Spawn["Use Codex spawn_agent"]
  IsCodex -->|No, OpenClaw or ACP session| Sessions["Use OpenClaw sessions_spawn"]
```

Severity: medium-high, but intentional.

Recommendation: keep this disambiguation in the Codex app-server developer instructions. Without it, Codex sees two delegation surfaces and can choose the wrong one.

### 4. Lightweight Codex context versus AGENTS.md

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  Light["OpenClaw lightweight context"] --> Config["project_doc_max_bytes = 0"]
  Config --> CodexDocs["Codex native AGENTS.md loading disabled"]
  Bootstrap["OpenClaw bootstrap text"] --> Claim["Claims Codex loads AGENTS.md natively"]
  CodexDocs --> Conflict["Conflict"]
  Claim --> Conflict
```

Severity: high when lightweight mode is used.

Recommendation: do not claim Codex loads AGENTS.md if the thread config disables project docs, or inject equivalent scoped AGENTS content when lightweight mode intentionally suppresses native docs. This is a prompt-surface correctness issue, not a PI prompt issue.

### 5. Skills and user-editable context priority

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  OpenClawSkills["OpenClaw skillsSnapshot.prompt"] --> Dev["Codex developerInstructions"]
  CodexSkills["Codex native skills instructions"] --> DevBundle["Codex developer bundle"]
  WorkspaceContext["SOUL/MEMORY/HEARTBEAT/project files"] --> Dev
  Dev --> Risk["Risk: duplicated or elevated user-editable context"]
  DevBundle --> Risk
```

Severity: medium.

Recommendation: keep OpenClaw-only context, but avoid turning generic user-editable context into Codex developer policy unless that priority is intentional. This is a good follow-up area after the prompt-surface routing split is in place.

### 6. Legacy plugin guidance compatibility

Severity: medium-low.

Legacy string guidance remains global for backwards compatibility. New structured entries can be scoped by surface. This PR preserves old plugin behavior while giving new prompt callers a precise path.

## Scope Boundary

This PR should be reviewed as the prompt-surface split:

- In scope: explicit prompt surface kinds, scoped plugin command guidance, PI/CLI/subagent/Codex developer-instruction routing, and visible-reply delivery wording.
- In scope to harden before merge if needed: any direct contradiction that makes a non-PI surface render PI/OpenClaw workflow text.
- Follow-up: deeper native Codex context priority cleanup for OpenClaw skills, SOUL/MEMORY/HEARTBEAT placement, and any decision to suppress OpenClaw GPT-5 personality overlay on native Codex by default.
- Out of scope: harness selection policy, model routing, auth profile routing, Codex app-server transport behavior, ACP runtime behavior, and GitNexus indexing freshness.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: prompt routing now follows the selected runtime prompt surface instead of leaking PI/Codex/CLI/subagent assumptions through shared strings.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source worktree at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation`, branch `codex/prompt-surface-runtime-separation`, using the repo's TypeScript runtime loader (`node --import tsx`) against the actual prompt builders.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pwd
node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'EOF'
import { buildAgentSystemPrompt } from './src/agents/system-prompt.ts';
import { buildCliAgentSystemPrompt } from './src/agents/cli-runner/helpers.ts';
import { resolveAgentPromptSurfaceForSessionKey } from './src/agents/prompt-surface.ts';
import { buildDeveloperInstructions } from './extensions/codex/src/app-server/thread-lifecycle.ts';
import { clearPluginCommands, registerPluginCommand } from './src/plugins/commands.ts';

const workspaceDir = process.cwd();
clearPluginCommands();
registerPluginCommand('probe-plugin', {
  name: 'probe_cmd',
  description: 'Probe command',
  agentPromptGuidance: [
    { text: 'PI main probe guidance.', surfaces: ['pi_main'] },
    { text: 'CLI backend probe guidance.', surfaces: ['cli_backend'] },
    { text: 'Codex app-server probe guidance.', surfaces: ['codex_app_server'] },
    { text: 'ACP backend probe guidance.', surfaces: ['acp_backend'] },
    { text: 'Subagent probe guidance.', surfaces: ['subagent'] },
  ],
  handler: async () => ({ text: 'ok' }),
});
const piPrompt = buildAgentSystemPrompt({ workspaceDir, toolNames: [], promptSurface: 'pi_main', nativeCommandGuidanceLines: ['PI main probe guidance.'] });
const cliPrompt = buildCliAgentSystemPrompt({ workspaceDir, tools: [], modelDisplay: 'test/model' });
const acpSurface = resolveAgentPromptSurfaceForSessionKey('agent:codex:acp:probe');
const acpPrompt = buildAgentSystemPrompt({ workspaceDir, toolNames: [], promptSurface: acpSurface, nativeCommandGuidanceLines: ['ACP backend probe guidance.'] });
const subagentPrompt = buildAgentSystemPrompt({ workspaceDir, toolNames: [], promptSurface: 'subagent', nativeCommandGuidanceLines: ['Subagent probe guidance.'] });
const baseCodexParams = {
  agentDir: workspaceDir,
  workspaceDir,
  provider: 'openai-codex-responses',
  modelId: 'gpt-5.4-codex',
  config: {},
  prompt: 'probe prompt',
};
const messageToolOnlyDeveloper = buildDeveloperInstructions({
  ...baseCodexParams,
  sourceReplyDeliveryMode: 'message_tool_only',
});
const automaticDeveloper = buildDeveloperInstructions({
  ...baseCodexParams,
  sourceReplyDeliveryMode: 'automatic',
});

const probe = {
  cwd: workspaceDir,
  piHasPiFallback: piPrompt.includes('Pi lists the standard tools above'),
  cliHasPiFallback: cliPrompt.includes('Pi lists the standard tools above'),
  cliHasBackendFallback: cliPrompt.includes('No OpenClaw tool list is injected for this runtime prompt surface'),
  cliHasLongWaitHint: cliPrompt.includes('For long waits'),
  cliHasSessionsSpawnHint: cliPrompt.includes('Larger work: use `sessions_spawn`'),
  cliHasPollingHint: cliPrompt.includes('Do not poll `subagents list` / `sessions_list` in a loop'),
  cliHasCliScopedGuidance: cliPrompt.includes('CLI backend probe guidance.'),
  cliHasPiScopedGuidance: cliPrompt.includes('PI main probe guidance.'),
  acpSurfaceFromSessionKey: acpSurface,
  acpHasAcpScopedGuidance: acpPrompt.includes('ACP backend probe guidance.'),
  acpHasPiScopedGuidance: acpPrompt.includes('PI main probe guidance.'),
  acpHasPiFallback: acpPrompt.includes('Pi lists the standard tools above'),
  subagentHasSubagentGuidance: subagentPrompt.includes('Subagent probe guidance.'),
  subagentHasPiGuidance: subagentPrompt.includes('PI main probe guidance.'),
  messageToolOnlySaysUseMessage: messageToolOnlyDeveloper.includes('Visible channel replies: use `message`'),
  automaticSaysUseMessage: automaticDeveloper.includes('Visible channel replies: use `message`'),
  automaticUsesActiveDeliveryPath: automaticDeveloper.includes('Visible channel replies should use the active Codex delivery path'),
  codexHasCodexScopedGuidance: automaticDeveloper.includes('Codex app-server probe guidance.'),
  codexHasPiScopedGuidance: automaticDeveloper.includes('PI main probe guidance.'),
  codexHasNativeSpawnAgentGuidance: automaticDeveloper.includes('Use Codex native `spawn_agent` for Codex subagents'),
};
console.log(JSON.stringify(probe, null, 2));
clearPluginCommands();
EOF
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the live prompt probe:

```text
/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation
{
  "cwd": "/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-prompt-surface-runtime-separation",
  "piHasPiFallback": true,
  "cliHasPiFallback": false,
  "cliHasBackendFallback": true,
  "cliHasLongWaitHint": false,
  "cliHasSessionsSpawnHint": false,
  "cliHasPollingHint": false,
  "cliHasCliScopedGuidance": true,
  "cliHasPiScopedGuidance": false,
  "acpSurfaceFromSessionKey": "acp_backend",
  "acpHasAcpScopedGuidance": true,
  "acpHasPiScopedGuidance": false,
  "acpHasPiFallback": false,
  "subagentHasSubagentGuidance": true,
  "subagentHasPiGuidance": false,
  "messageToolOnlySaysUseMessage": true,
  "automaticSaysUseMessage": false,
  "automaticUsesActiveDeliveryPath": true,
  "codexHasCodexScopedGuidance": true,
  "codexHasPiScopedGuidance": false,
  "codexHasNativeSpawnAgentGuidance": true
}
```

Observed result after fix: the rendered PI prompt still includes the PI empty-tool fallback; the rendered CLI backend prompt does not include PI wording or OpenClaw long-wait/session-spawn/polling workflow hints when no tool list is injected; ACP session keys resolve to the `acp_backend` surface and render ACP-scoped guidance without PI guidance or fallback text; CLI, subagent, and Codex app-server scoped command guidance render only on their matching surfaces; Codex `message_tool_only` developer instructions still say to use `message`, while Codex `automatic` developer instructions use the active delivery path instead of a hard `message` instruction.

What was not tested: full repository test suite, live Codex app-server connection to Codex Desktop, GitNexus refreshed index.

## Verification

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/plugins/commands.test.ts src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/system-prompt.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/helpers.system-prompt.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-subpaths.test.ts
git diff --check
```

- Focused prompt/registry/compaction/Codex/plugin-SDK tests passed 5 Vitest shards in 31.68s after the adversarial-review hardening fixes.
- `git diff --check` passed.

## Adversarial Sweep

Four read-only adversarial review passes covered prompt-surface correctness, lifecycle/compaction routing, tests/mocks, and public SDK/API compatibility. Two findings survived manual verification and were fixed in this head:

- P1 bundled startup safety: avoid a module-scope prompt-surface `Set` in `command-registration.ts`, matching the file's existing lazy-init TDZ guard pattern.
- P2 subagent guidance placement: render subagent-scoped command guidance independently of ACP-specific routing text, with regression coverage for ACP-disabled subagent prompts.

A second Codex-native conflict audit compared OpenClaw's Codex bridge to the actual Codex app-server prompt stack. That audit produced the scenarios documented above: native Codex owns base/model/personality/AGENTS/native skills, while OpenClaw should own delivery routing, dynamic-tool routing, ACP/session disambiguation, and genuinely OpenClaw-only context.

target.title: feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall
target.body:
# PR 1 draft: Feishu agent message delete and recall

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall actions
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_message` tool for `list`, `delete`, `recall`, `read_receipts`, and `read_users`.
- Wire Feishu `messages` tool config through defaults, schema validation, plugin metadata, API exports, and runtime registration.
- Expose Telegram-like generic message actions for Feishu: `delete` and `unsend`.
- Track the latest bot-sent Feishu message per account/chat so natural-language requests like "delete your previous message" can work even when the model omits `messageId`.
- Document Feishu message-management permissions, Unix-second time filters, and delete/recall behavior.

## Related upstream work

- Closes / revives https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51422
- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/52993
- Complements, but does not replace, inbound recall-event work in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/55225 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/55691
- Leaves Feishu reaction tool registration for a follow-up PR, because reaction fixes are already active in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Fixes compared with #52993

- `start_time` and `end_time` are documented and validated as Unix-second timestamp strings, matching Feishu's API, instead of ISO 8601 strings.
- `delete` and `recall` have distinct request paths and user-facing messages; delete no longer reuses recall semantics such as "cannot recall after two minutes".
- Missing Feishu scopes are surfaced with actionable messages, including `im:message.group_msg` for group-message deletion.

## Notes for reviewers

- Feishu delete behaves as a platform soft-delete/recall: follow-up reads can return `deleted: true`, `updated: true`, and empty content. Some clients may still show a deletion placeholder.
- The last-bot-message fallback is intentionally in-memory and scoped by account/chat with a 24-hour TTL. It only records messages sent by this bot process.
- The feature is gated by `tools.messages`, defaulting to `false` because message list/delete/recall/read-receipts are sensitive.

## Testing

```text
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/message-tool.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.reply-fallback.test.ts
# 4 files, 104 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts
# 2 files, 84 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
# 63 files, 786 tests passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
# passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxlint extensions/feishu/src/sent-message-cache.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
# passed, 0 warnings/errors

git diff --check
# passed
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can delete or recall bot messages from a real group chat through the new `feishu_message` tool and generic `message(action="delete"|"unsend")` path.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw gateway from this branch, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scopes including `im:message:update`, `im:message:readonly`, and `im:message.group_msg`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enabled the opt-in message management gate for the Feishu test account, started the local gateway from this branch with `openclaw gateway run --force`, sent messages in the real Feishu group, first verified delete failed before `im:message.group_msg` was granted, granted the scope and restarted the gateway, sent a live bot reply, asked the bot to delete its previous message, then checked the deleted message through Feishu message read output.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output and redacted runtime notes from the real Feishu setup:

```text
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Feishu WebSocket ready at 2026-05-18T15:43:04+08:00

Initial group delete attempt:
  Feishu API error code=230027
  msg="need scope: im:message.group_msg"
  log_id=20260518150333B37818A84E685EE4FE60

After granting im:message.group_msg:
  sent message_id=om_x100b6f9fbdc2c8e4c3bb0f1440e9c1d
  im.message.delete response={"code":0,"msg":"success"}
  follow-up message read:
    deleted=true
    updated=true
    content_length=0

Natural-language group flow after last-bot-message fallback:
  user: delete your previous message
  agent called message(action="delete") without messageId
  fallback resolved last bot message in chat oc_* to om_*
  Feishu delete returned deleted=true
```

Observed result after fix: The bot replied in the Feishu group, deleted its own previous message after the required group-message scope was enabled, and Feishu readback showed the target message was deleted with empty content. The natural-language "delete your previous message" case also worked without the model supplying a `messageId`.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

target.title: feat(approvals): add plain-language plugin approvals
target.body:
## Mission

Plugin approvals are a security boundary, but they are often delivered in chat where the old fallback text read like internal debug output. A normal approval could show a raw title, full command text, proposed exec policy, session key, tool/plugin IDs, expiry, and `/approve <id>` syntax all at once.

That raw format is useful for maintainers, but it is not easy for a Telegram/Matrix user to scan quickly. The goal of this PR is to make opt-in plugin approvals understandable at a glance while preserving the legacy raw behavior by default.

In short: keep the original technical approval prompt as the default, and add a plain-language approval mode for users who explicitly choose it.

## What This Adds

This PR adds `approvals.plugin.language` with three modes:

- `original`: existing raw technical plugin approval text. This remains the default.
- `simple`: a short plain-language prompt with `Action`, `It will`, `Risk`, and available choices.
- `simple-technical`: the plain-language prompt plus the raw technical fields below it.

Example config:

```json
{
  "approvals": {
    "plugin": {
      "language": "simple"
    }
  }
}
```

The new renderer keeps interactive approval metadata intact so native Telegram/Matrix approval buttons can still be rendered. Text-only channels still get an actionable `/approve plugin:<id> allow-once|allow-always|deny` fallback.

## Implementation

- Added the typed `PluginApprovalLanguage` config surface and validation.
- Wired the configured language through shared plugin approval rendering.
- Forwarded the language option through fallback delivery and native Telegram/Matrix approval payloads.
- Added plain-English approval output with risk text and decision-aware fallback instructions.
- Added `Command preview` output for commands that are high risk or cannot be summarized completely.
- Redacted token/password/API-key/bearer/basic-auth/cookie/userinfo forms from previews before they can be posted into chat.
- Updated generated config and plugin SDK API baselines.
- Added focused renderer and forwarder tests.

## Safety Model

The important rule is: if `simple` mode hides raw technical details, the summary must either explain the meaningful action or fail closed.

Fail closed means the approval becomes higher risk and shows a redacted command preview instead of pretending the command is harmless.

The renderer now handles and tests common Codex app-server command shapes:

- shell wrappers: `bash -lc`, `sh -c`, `zsh -c`, `env`, `timeout`, `set -a`, `source`;
- shell control: `&&`, `||`, `;`, background `&`, conditionals, loops, and attached redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`;
- pipelines: known stages are summarized stage-by-stage; unknown stages become high risk with a preview;
- file reads/writes/deletes: `cat`, `sed`, `find`, `rm`, `mkdir`, `touch`, `cp`, `mv`, redirection writes;
- network transfers: `curl` and `wget` URL targets, upload operands, output operands, stdin upload via redirection, config/header/cookie/auth/netrc forms;
- credentials: bearer headers, OAuth bearer flags, basic auth, proxy auth, cookie headers, netrc credential sources, URL userinfo;
- dev/ops commands: `git`, `docker`, service managers, package managers, interpreters, logs/process inspection;
- remote commands: `ssh`, `scp`, SSH remote commands, and SSH local-execution options such as `ProxyCommand` and `LocalCommand`.

## Review And Hardening Trail

This PR went through a long approval-safety review loop because turning verbose technical prompts into plain language makes the summarizer security-sensitive.

Issues fixed during review:

- Preserved actionable text fallback instructions for channels without buttons.
- Made pipelines visible before raw command text can be hidden.
- Made command target extraction command-aware so boolean flags like `cat -n .env` do not hide sensitive targets.
- Surfaced curl/wget upload and output operands, including `.env` style local-file transfers.
- Handled stdin uploads such as `curl --data-binary @- ... < .env`.
- Redacted basic-auth, OAuth bearer, cookie, header, and URL credential forms from command previews.
- Failed closed on curl config files and curl header-file inputs.
- Surfaced wget credential/header/cookie options.
- Showed targets for redirected formatter writes like `printf ... > .env`.
- Handled background shell separators such as `sleep 1 & rm -rf /tmp/x`.
- Parsed Git global options such as `git -C repo reset --hard` before classifying subcommands.
- Treated `find` output-file predicates like `-fprint`, `-fprint0`, `-fls`, and `-fprintf` as writes.
- Detected attached shell redirections like `cat notes.txt>out.txt`.
- Failed closed on SSH local-execution options such as `ssh -o ProxyCommand=...` and `ssh -o LocalCommand=...`.
- Refreshed against latest `upstream/main` repeatedly and regenerated the config baseline after conflicts.
- Stabilized cron test mock state exposed by the refreshed CI shard.

The latest ClawSweeper review no longer reports a concrete repair item. It now asks for human maintainer review of the opt-in approval-safety boundary and branch scope.

## Current Status

- Latest head at time of this update: `91d6303a0e`.
- GitHub reports the PR mergeable and clean.
- CI/checks are green on the latest head.
- `Real behavior proof` is successful.
- ClawSweeper durable review status: needs maintainer review before merge, not an automated repair.
- Remaining decision: maintainers need to accept the opt-in plain-language approval UX/security boundary, or ask for scope splitting if they want the unrelated refreshed-base/test-stability churn separated.

## Verification

Local verification after the latest SSH hardening and main refresh:

- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts src/plugin-sdk/approval-renderers.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.infra.config.ts src/infra/plugin-approval-forwarder.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm check:test-types`
- `pnpm lint --threads=8`

Earlier focused verification also covered config schema/help quality, plugin SDK API baseline, gateway/channel state, and live renderer output.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Chat-delivered plugin approvals can now render as a short plain-English approval card instead of a raw technical dump when `approvals.plugin.language` is set to `simple`. The old raw format remains the default through `original`.
- Real environment tested: Hadi's live OpenClaw source checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 (`/home/hadi/src/openclaw`), the PR worktree, and Telegram channel status in polling mode.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - Rendered real approval messages through `buildPluginApprovalRequestMessage(..., { language: "simple" })`.
  - Ran focused approval renderer and forwarder tests.
  - Ran type, lint, config docs, and baseline checks.
  - Cherry-picked the feature into the live source checkout during testing, ran `pnpm build`, restarted `openclaw-gateway.service`, and checked gateway/Telegram channel state.
- Evidence after fix: terminal renderer output showed the intended card shape:

```text
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Create/check workspace files or folders

It will
- create folder(s): memory
- check whether a condition or file exists: memory/context.md

Risk: Low
Only creates or checks workspace files. No network, service, or delete step detected.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:example allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Pipeline and unknown-stage proof:

```text
--- pipeline curl ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Use the network or download data

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- make a network request or download data: https://example.test/upload

Risk: Medium
Network commands can send or fetch data outside this machine.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:pipeline allow-once|allow-always|deny

--- unknown pipeline ---
🛡️ Approval needed
Action
Run a terminal command

It will
- read file contents: notes.txt
- run custom-uploader

Command preview
cat notes.txt | custom-uploader --send

Risk: High
This command is part of a pipeline I cannot fully summarize, so review it before approving.

Choose below.
If buttons are unavailable, reply: /approve plugin:unknown allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

Live channel state checked during proof:

```text
Telegram default (Telegram): enabled, configured, running, connected, mode:polling, token:tokenFile
```

Observed result after fix:

- `simple` mode no longer includes the raw technical details block.
- Escaped shell separators such as `&&` are decoded before parsing.
- Shell conditionals no longer surface as fake `run if` / `run then` actions.
- Non-shell pipeline stages are visible or fail closed with a high-risk preview.
- Sensitive command previews are redacted before being shown in chat.
- SSH local-execution options now fail closed with a high-risk preview.

- What was not tested:

- The Mantis Telegram Desktop GIF run skipped instead of producing before/after GIFs. This PR still has terminal renderer proof, live gateway/channel state proof, earlier Telegram screenshot proof, and a successful `Real behavior proof` check.

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve raw external session aliases
target.body:
Closes #63005.

## Behavior addressed
Stable raw external session keys such as `conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123` should keep resolving to the same OpenClaw session after gateway canonicalization and nested `sessions_send` / agent activity.

## Root cause
The gateway session-store migration path resolved the raw external key to the canonical `agent:<id>:...` key, copied the freshest entry to the canonical key, then pruned the raw key as a legacy alias. Future inbound traffic that still looked up the raw external key could create a fresh `sessionId`, splitting one logical conversation.

## Fix
`migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey` now preserves non-agent structured raw external aliases while still pruning true legacy aliases. The regression test seeds the reported raw key shape and verifies the raw key and canonical key both retain `sess-raw`.

## Evidence
Environment: source checkout, Node `v22.22.0`, rebased onto current `upstream/main`.

Current PR head: `87ecc789b95a4aeab08c89fa86d7c4eacc607cf7`  
Current base checked: `3b5d30b5fdfd2d9e1e2f1c97242444467dce53c0`

Commands run after rebase:

```sh
pnpm docs:list
git rebase upstream/main
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts
```

Focused test result:

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       264 passed (264)
Duration    24.17s
```

Formatter result:

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
```

Screenshots: not applicable; this is backend session-store behavior. Terminal proof above covers the exact code path and regression.

## Not tested
Live third-party integration traffic was not exercised. The regression targets the deterministic session-store helper path implicated by the issue.

target.title: fix(gateway): report unqueued restart continuations
target.body:
Closes #74424.

## Behavior addressed
A gateway restart request with `continuationMessage` should not silently imply that the continuation was queued when the restart coalesces with an already-emitted restart where hooks cannot run.

## Root cause
`scheduleGatewaySigusr1Restart()` returned the coalesced success shape from the already-emitted `hasUnconsumedRestartSignal()` branch before registering or running `emitHooks`. The gateway tool only writes the restart sentinel from `emitHooks.beforeEmit`, so a continuation could be accepted by the tool call but never persisted for post-restart delivery.

## Fix
Add `emitHooksQueued` to the internal scheduled-restart result and expose `continuationQueued` from the gateway restart tool when a continuation payload is present. This keeps the fix narrow: it reports the unqueued continuation explicitly instead of attempting to merge a new continuation into an already-emitted restart.

## Evidence
Environment: source checkout, Node `v22.22.0`, rebased onto current `upstream/main`.

Current PR head: `e15e7f920a95bcb257fd111c64c59534ff0ec1c8`  
Current base checked: `3b5d30b5fdfd2d9e1e2f1c97242444467dce53c0`

Commands run after rebase:

```sh
pnpm docs:list
git rebase upstream/main
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts
git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/restart.ts src/infra/infra-runtime.test.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.test.ts
```

Focused test result:

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests       42 passed (42)
Duration    2.46s
```

Formatter result:

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
```

Screenshots: not applicable; this is backend scheduler/tool-result behavior. Terminal proof above covers the exact coalesced restart branch and gateway tool response.

## Not tested
A full live gateway restart and channel-visible continuation delivery were not exercised. The tests cover the scheduler branch where hooks cannot be queued and the gateway tool result that reports `continuationQueued: false`.

target.title: Fix Codex runtime hook and session churn
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes two Codex-runtime stability problems reported by users:

- Fixes #76552: Codex no longer installs the default `PreToolUse` native hook relay command when no `before_tool_call` policy exists, avoiding one `openclaw hooks relay ... --event pre_tool_use` child process per native Codex tool call.
- Fixes #76179: Codex keeps owner-only dynamic tool schemas stable across sender-owner flips, so app-server threads resume instead of constantly starting fresh native Codex sessions.

## Root Cause

For #76552, current `main` already avoided relay subprocesses for `PostToolUse` and finalization hooks when no local handlers exist, but `pre_tool_use` still returned `true` unconditionally in `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts`. That meant default Codex app-server config could still register a `PreToolUse` command hook that shells out through the OpenClaw relay for every native tool use, even when there was no OpenClaw `before_tool_call` policy to run.

For #76179, the Codex app-server thread fingerprint included the dynamic tool registration set. Owner-only tools were filtered out for non-owner senders, so an owner/non-owner context change could alter the registered dynamic tool names and force a `thread/start` instead of `thread/resume`. Since Codex receives dynamic tool schemas on thread start, not as a cheap per-turn update, this made a transient availability flip expensive and session-churning.

## What Changed

- `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.ts` now only reports local `pre_tool_use` relay work when `hasBeforeToolCallPolicy()` is true. `permission_request` remains active because Codex approvals still need the app-server bridge.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts` opts Codex dynamic tool construction into stable owner-only schema registration.
- `src/agents/tool-policy.ts` can retain unavailable owner-only tools while wrapping their execution with a fail-closed authorization guard.
- `src/agents/pi-tools.ts` passes the new retain option through to the owner-only policy layer.

## Before / After

Before:

- Default Codex sessions could register a `PreToolUse` relay command even with no `before_tool_call` plugin policy, so multiple native tools could fan out into multiple OpenClaw relay CLI child processes.
- Switching from an owner sender to a non-owner sender could change dynamic tool registration and force a new Codex native thread.

After:

- No `PreToolUse` relay command is installed unless there is actual before-tool policy work.
- Command approvals still get the in-process `nativeHookRelay` bridge for `pre_tool_use` decisions.
- Codex dynamic tool schemas stay stable across owner-only availability flips, but unauthorized owner-only tool execution still throws `Tool restricted to owner senders.`
- Owner to non-owner turns reuse the same Codex thread via `thread/resume`.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex runtime process/session churn. Specifically: default app-server runs should not install a `PreToolUse` relay command when no `before_tool_call` policy exists, and owner-only dynamic tool availability changes should not force a fresh Codex native thread while unauthorized owner-only execution still fails closed.
- Real environment tested: Local macOS OpenClaw checkout; live Codex proof was captured at PR head `484cf48b97`, then the current rebased head `5238e8638d` was verified again on May 18, 2026 against green `main` `491ce8b753`. Node `v22.22.0`, Codex CLI logged in with ChatGPT auth, OpenClaw bundled `codex` plugin loaded from this branch. Private home/workspace paths and auth details redacted below.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran two real local Codex app-server OpenClaw turns on the same session, then inspected the redacted trajectory/sidecar, and ran a throwaway production-code runtime probe with `node --import tsx` to print the hook config, owner-only tool policy result, and thread lifecycle request sequence.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

```text
$ codex login status
Logged in using ChatGPT

$ OPENCLAW_LOG_LEVEL=debug pnpm openclaw agent --local --model openai/gpt-5.4 --session-id codex-runtime-proof-83367 --message "Reply with exactly: OK" --json --timeout 180

[plugins] loaded 1 plugin(s) (1 attempted) in 210.5ms
[agents/auth-profiles] used external cli oauth bootstrap because local oauth was missing or unusable
[agent/embedded] strict-agentic execution contract active: runId=codex-runtime-proof-83367 sessionId=codex-runtime-proof-83367 provider=openai-codex/gpt-5.4 harness=codex configured=unspecified
[agents/harness] agent harness selected
...

$ OPENCLAW_LOG_LEVEL=debug pnpm openclaw agent --local --model openai/gpt-5.4 --session-id codex-runtime-proof-83367 --message "Reply with exactly: OK2" --json --timeout 180

...

$ jq -r 'select(.type=="session.started" or .type=="prompt.submitted" or .type=="model.completed" or .type=="session.ended") | [.type, .ts, (.data.threadId // ""), (.data.turnId // ""), (.data.assistantTexts[0] // .data.status // "")] | @tsv' <redacted-session>.trajectory.jsonl

model.completed  2026-05-18T01:38:02.058Z  019e38bb-8c64-74a0-b5f5-fbc99f141e39  019e38bb-8c82-7ff1-91cd-3723111902c0  OK

$ jq '{threadId, model, modelProvider, createdAt, updatedAt, dynamicToolsFingerprintPresent:(.dynamicToolsFingerprint!=null), sessionFile:"<redacted-session-file>"}' <redacted-session>.jsonl.codex-app-server.json

  "model": "gpt-5.4",
  "modelProvider": null,
  "createdAt": "2026-05-18T01:37:55.835Z",

```

Production-code runtime probe output:

```json
{
  "hookRelay": {
    "preToolUse": [],
    "postToolUse": [],
    "stop": [],
    "permissionRequestCommandIncludes": {
      "permission_request": true,
      "pre_tool_use": false
    }
  },

      "cron",

    ],

    ],

    "unauthorizedCronResult": "Tool restricted to owner senders."
  },
  "threadLifecycle": {
    "requestMethods": [
      "thread/start",

    ],

      null,

    ],

      [
        "cron",

        "nodes"

      []
    ]
  }

```

- Observed result after fix: The real Codex app-server OpenClaw session completed two live `openai-codex/gpt-5.4` turns with the same native thread id. The runtime probe shows default hook config sends `hooks.PreToolUse: []` while keeping `PermissionRequest` relay active, retained owner-only tool names are stable for owner and non-owner policy inputs, unauthorized owner-only execution throws `Tool restricted to owner senders.`, and lifecycle calls are `thread/start` followed by `thread/resume`.
- What was not tested: I did not run a live external chat-channel ingress scenario that naturally flips `senderIsOwner`; that path needs real channel identity setup. The same owner/non-owner availability flip was exercised through the production owner-only policy and Codex thread lifecycle code path in the runtime probe above, and the local live Codex session verified the actual app-server harness/auth path.

## Tests / Proof

No product UI screenshot is applicable here because the bugs are process/session churn in the Codex runtime path. I included terminal proof so reviewers can see exactly what was exercised.

Current-head verification after rebasing onto green `main` on May 18, 2026:

~~~text
$ git rev-parse HEAD
5238e8638db26d74db936d95536304848754604c

$ git rev-parse upstream/main
491ce8b7535baadbb73191587453ac1124c9b6c2

$ git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD
# no output

$ env PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:/Users/stainlu/.npm-global/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin pnpm test src/commands/agent.test.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts src/agents/pi-tools.owner-only-gating.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.model-overrides.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent.run-timeout-override.test.ts

[test] passed 4 Vitest shards in 66.84s
~~~

The type-test gate that had been red on the older upstream base is now green:

~~~text
$ env PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:/Users/stainlu/.npm-global/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin pnpm check:test-types
$ pnpm tsgo:test
$ pnpm tsgo:core:test && pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
~~~

The prior `checks-node-core-runtime-shared` CI failure was checked against the same Node 24 job shape locally and passed after the latest rebase:

~~~text
$ env PATH=/opt/homebrew/bin:/Users/stainlu/.npm-global/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=2 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=2 npx --yes node@24 scripts/test-projects.mjs test/vitest/vitest.acp.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.shared-core.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.utils.config.ts

[test] passed 5 Vitest shards in 56.52s; Vitest summaries above are per-shard, not aggregate totals.
~~~

Specific regressions covered by this PR:

- `src/agents/harness/native-hook-relay.test.ts`: no-handler relay state no longer enables `pre_tool_use`; `before_tool_call` still enables `PreToolUse`; `after_tool_call` does not accidentally enable `PreToolUse`.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`: default Codex thread config has `hooks.PreToolUse: []`, command approvals still carry the `nativeHookRelay` bridge, unavailable owner-only tools are retained for Codex fingerprints, and an owner/non-owner flip produces `thread/start` then `thread/resume`.
- `src/agents/pi-tools.owner-only-gating.test.ts`: retained owner-only tools remain registered for schema stability but reject execution for unauthorized senders.
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/side-question.test.ts`: side-question/permission-request coverage keeps permission relay behavior while confirming default `PreToolUse` stays empty when no before-tool policy exists.

Broad Testbox `pnpm check:changed` was not run because the Blacksmith/Testbox CLI/env/profile were not available in this environment; the focused affected suites, the type-test gate, and the Node 24 runtime-shared CI-shape run above are green locally. The latest push also triggers exact-head GitHub CI.

target.title: fix: harden release stability recovery and auth fallback
target.body:
## Why

This is a narrow release-stability bundle for operators who need a branch they can run for 1-2 weeks while the broader OpenClaw mainline settles.

It deliberately avoids a full Codex-harness migration. The patch only carries targeted recovery/visibility fixes for failures already seen in the unstable release path:

- stuck sessions queued behind terminal-looking embedded progress
- stale idle embedded-run lanes with queued work
- stale `model_call` / tool activity masking an active embedded run from idle queued recovery
- stale OpenAI Codex OAuth `lastGood` pointers after `refresh_token_reused`
- operator visibility for runtime-policy session aliases and Codex-backed runtime auth routes

LCM remains out of scope here because that fix is tracked separately in `lossless-claw`.

## Issue Links

- Fixes / complements #71127.
  - This PR includes the small safe pieces from #82733 plus the missing stale `model_call` and idle queued embedded-owner recovery gates.
  - It does not change channel/plugin startup semantics or command-lane scheduling policy.
- Fixes #79021 by clearing only the matching stale `lastGood` profile after `refresh_token_reused`, reloading the runtime store, and preserving the actual healthy fallback profile id for later success marking.
- References #83484 by exposing the runtime-policy session alias without changing session persistence.
- References #83485 by showing `auth.runtimeAuthRoutes` for Codex-backed OpenAI routes.

## What Changed

### Stuck-session recovery

- `src/logging/diagnostic.ts`
  - recovers idle queued embedded-run stalls when progress has gone stale
  - treats `hasActiveEmbeddedRun` as the ownership guard when the visible active work is `model_call` or `tool_call`
  - allows stale `model_call` activity to request active abort only when the activity snapshot still has an active embedded-run owner
  - passes the expected diagnostic state into recovery requests

- `src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.ts`
  - exposes `hasActiveEmbeddedRun` in the activity snapshot so model-call recovery remains scoped to embedded-run work

- `src/logging/diagnostic-session-recovery*.ts`
  - accepts expected `idle` recovery state
  - records queued lane work on abort outcomes
  - preserves real queued idle work when abort/reset releases active lane work

### Auth fallback

- `src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.ts`
  - on `refresh_token_reused`, clears only a matching stale `lastGood` pointer
  - resolves the persisted owner store for inherited profiles
  - patches the requesting runtime snapshot when needed
  - reloads the runtime store before choosing a legacy-default fallback
  - returns the actual fallback profile id/type so provider-level auth does not re-mark the stale profile as successful

- `src/agents/auth-profiles/profiles.ts`
  - adds the locked `clearLastGoodProfileWithLock` helper

### Operator visibility

- `src/commands/models/list.status-command.ts`
  - adds `auth.runtimeAuthRoutes` to JSON output
  - adds a human `Runtime auth` section for Codex-backed OpenAI text routes
  - reports compatible OpenAI API-key profile auth as the effective Codex runtime route source instead of `status=usable` plus `effective=missing`

- `src/commands/sessions.ts` and `src/commands/sessions-table.ts`
  - derives and displays a `runtimePolicySessionKey` alias for collapsed external direct sessions
  - keeps the persisted row key unchanged

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Release-stability operators need stuck OpenClaw sessions to become recoverable instead of requiring gateway restarts, need stale Codex OAuth `lastGood` loops to fall through to healthy profiles without re-poisoning `lastGood`, need collapsed external-DM sessions to expose their runtime-policy alias, and need `models status --json` to show trustworthy Codex-backed OpenAI auth routes.
- Real environment tested: Real OpenClaw CLI entrypoint from this checkout on local macOS, with `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-release-stability-state` so existing `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` did not affect the result. Focused diagnostic/auth/status regression commands were also run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-release-stability-fixes` after the adversarial-review follow-up commit `b5da773c59`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-release-stability-state \
OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 \
pnpm openclaw sessions --json \
  --store /Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-release-stability-sessions-proof.json \
  --limit 1

OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-release-stability-state \
OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 \
pnpm openclaw models status --json

OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.openai-codex-refresh-fallback.test.ts \
  src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts \
  src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts

OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.logging.config.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.integration.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.test.ts
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied live terminal output from the real OpenClaw CLI smoke showed the runtime-policy alias and Codex runtime auth route:

```json
{
  "path": "/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/openclaw-release-stability-sessions-proof.json",
  "count": 1,
  "sessions": [
    {
      "key": "agent:main:main",
      "sessionId": "release-stability-proof",
      "agentRuntime": {
        "id": "codex",
        "source": "implicit"
      },
      "runtimePolicySessionKey": "agent:main:telegram:default:direct:42"
    }
  ]
}
```

```json
{
  "auth": {
    "missingProvidersInUse": ["openai"],
    "runtimeAuthRoutes": [
      {
        "provider": "openai",
        "runtime": "codex",
        "authProvider": "openai-codex",
        "status": "missing",
        "effective": {
          "kind": "missing",
          "detail": "missing"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

Focused regression output after the adversarial-review fixes:

```text
Test Files  5 passed (5)
Tests  165 passed (165)

Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  64 passed (64)

Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
```

- Observed result after fix: `sessions --json` returned the persisted row key `agent:main:main` plus `runtimePolicySessionKey: "agent:main:telegram:default:direct:42"`; `models status --json` returned `auth.runtimeAuthRoutes[0]` showing `provider: "openai"`, `runtime: "codex"`, `authProvider: "openai-codex"`, and `status: "missing"` for the isolated no-auth state. Focused regressions proved that idle queued embedded-run stalls request `expectedState: "idle"`, stale `model_call` recovery is gated by `hasActiveEmbeddedRun`, idle queued work still recovers when embedded ownership is surfaced as `model_call`, ordinary stale model calls without embedded ownership do not abort, queued idle work is preserved when `queuedCount > 0`, stale `openai-codex:default` `refresh_token_reused` clears matching `lastGood`, provider-level auth reports the healthy fallback profile id, and compatible OpenAI API-key profiles no longer produce contradictory runtime-auth status.
- What was not tested: No live Telegram message was sent, no LCM behavior was exercised, no channel/plugin startup semantics were changed, and no blanket auth-route rewrite for `openai/gpt-5.5` was attempted.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): The linked reports #71127 and #79021 contain the before-fix symptoms. #71127 shows queued sessions stuck behind terminal embedded progress with `recovery=none`; #79021 shows `openai-codex` `refresh_token_reused` loops while stale `lastGood.openai-codex` still points at the failed profile.

## Validation

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.openai-codex-refresh-fallback.test.ts \
  src/agents/model-auth.profiles.test.ts \
  src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts
```

Result: `5 passed`, `165 passed`.

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.logging.config.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.runtime.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-stuck-session-recovery.integration.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic-run-activity.test.ts
```

Result: `3 passed`, `64 passed`.

```bash
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.openai-codex-refresh-fallback.test.ts \
  src/agents/auth-profiles/oauth.ts \
  src/agents/model-auth.ts \
  src/commands/models/list.status-command.ts \
  src/commands/models/list.status.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts \
  src/logging/diagnostic.ts
```

Result: all matched files formatted.

```bash
git diff --check
```

Result: clean.

## Adversarial Review

Four read-only subagents reviewed the PR by slice: auth fallback/token-burn, diagnostic recovery/queue safety, models/sessions observability, and test/release-branch scope. Confirmed findings fixed in `b5da773c59`:

- P1: provider-level auth could return a healthy fallback token while still attributing success to stale `openai-codex:default`, re-poisoning `lastGood`.
- P1: idle queued recovery only recognized `activeWorkKind=embedded_run`, missing stale embedded-owned work surfaced as `model_call` or `tool_call`.
- P2: `models status` could show `status=usable` while `effective=missing` for compatible OpenAI API-key profiles used by Codex runtime routes.

## Non-Goals

- no LCM changes
- no Telegram/channel startup rewrite
- no command-lane scheduling rewrite
- no session persistence change

target.title: fix(provider-catalog): skip catalog discovery when apiKey is a non-secret auth marker
target.body:
## Summary

`buildSingleProviderApiKeyCatalog` and `buildPairedProviderApiKeyCatalog` in `src/plugins/provider-catalog.ts` only check `!apiKey` before allowing catalog discovery. When the resolved `apiKey` is a non-secret auth marker like `"custom-local"` or `"ollama-local"` (truthy strings), the guard passes, but `toDiscoveryApiKey()` filters these markers to `undefined`. The downstream `discoverOpenAICompatibleLocalModels()` then sends `GET /models` without any `Authorization` header.

## Root Cause

`model-auth-markers.ts` defines several non-secret markers used for local/self-hosted providers that don't require authentication:
- `CUSTOM_LOCAL_AUTH_MARKER = "custom-local"`
- `OLLAMA_LOCAL_AUTH_MARKER = "ollama-local"`
- etc.

`models-config.providers.secrets.ts` → `toDiscoveryApiKey()` correctly filters these to `undefined`. But the guard in `provider-catalog.ts` didn't account for this — it only checked falsiness, and markers are truthy strings.

## Fix

Added `isNonSecretApiKeyMarker(apiKey)` check to both catalog functions.

## Tracking

Closes #83461

target.title: fix(skill-creator): reject empty name and description in skill valida…
target.body:
## Summary

`validate_skill()` in `quick_validate.py` silently accepts empty or whitespace-only `name` and `description` values in SKILL.md frontmatter. The `if name:` guard skips all format checks when the value is an empty string, allowing invalid skill metadata to pass validation.

Since `name` and `description` are the fields OpenClaw reads to decide when a skill gets triggered, accepting blank values violates the documented metadata contract and breaks skill triggering and discovery behavior.

## Changes

**`skills/skill-creator/scripts/quick_validate.py`**
- Replace permissive `if name:` / `if description:` guards with early-return checks that reject empty/whitespace-only values
- Flattens validation logic by removing one level of nesting

**`skills/skill-creator/scripts/test_quick_validate.py`**
- Add 4 regression tests: empty name, whitespace-only name, empty description, whitespace-only description

## Testing

All 7 tests pass (3 existing + 4 new). See **Real behavior proof** section below for the run output.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `validate_skill()` in `quick_validate.py` accepts empty or whitespace-only `name` and `description` values in SKILL.md frontmatter, so a skill with `name: ""` or `description: "   "` passes validation and downstream packaging.

Real environment tested: Local `python3 quick_validate.py` against ephemeral SKILL.md fixtures on macOS arm64, comparing `origin/main` (commit 406ae72) against the fix branch. Also ran the colocated `test_quick_validate.py` unittest suite.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```
# Before fix (main): empty name silently accepted
$ echo '---
name: ""
description: a valid description
---' > SKILL.md
$ python3 quick_validate.py /path/to/test-skill

# After fix (this branch): four invalid cases
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # name: ""
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # name: "   "
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # description: ""
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # description: "   "

# Unit suite
$ python3 -m unittest skills/skill-creator/scripts/test_quick_validate.py
```

Evidence after fix:

```
# Before fix (main)
$ python3 quick_validate.py /path/to/test-skill
Skill is valid!     <- BUG: should reject empty name

# After fix (this branch)
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # name: ""
Name must not be empty
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # name: "   "
Name must not be empty
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # description: ""
Description must not be empty
$ python3 quick_validate.py   # description: "   "
Description must not be empty

# Unit tests
test_accepts_crlf_frontmatter ... ok
test_fallback_parser_handles_multiline_frontmatter_without_pyyaml ... ok
test_rejects_empty_description ... ok
test_rejects_empty_name ... ok
test_rejects_missing_frontmatter_closing_fence ... ok
test_rejects_whitespace_only_description ... ok
test_rejects_whitespace_only_name ... ok

Ran 7 tests in 0.003s -- OK
```

Observed result after fix: All four empty/whitespace cases produce `Name must not be empty` or `Description must not be empty` and `quick_validate.py` exits non-zero, instead of returning `Skill is valid!`. The four previously-existing tests still pass.

What was not tested: The change was not exercised end-to-end through `package_skill.py`'s real archive build (the validator gate fires before any packaging side effects, so the path is short-circuited deterministically); the regression coverage is at the unit-test layer.

target.title: Filter heartbeat response-tool transcript artifacts
target.body:
## Summary
- Filters full silent heartbeat transcript spans before normal prompt assembly, including helper tool calls/results, prompt-only interrupted wakes, pre-terminal assistant status text, and `heartbeat_respond notify:false`.
- Preserves visible heartbeat output only when it is actually visible: free-text alerts remain, and `heartbeat_respond notify:true` is kept only after a completed successful matching response-tool result.
- Fixes bundled LINE extension lint by using the public command definition type instead of deriving it through a broad API parameter.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Later normal prompts no longer receive stale silent heartbeat artifacts such as `[OpenClaw heartbeat poll]`, `HEARTBEAT.md`, `heartbeat_respond`, or `notify=false`; the next real user prompt remains available for the provider context.
Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on this PR branch at `20b0b80da792e128bb63d26d4bdcbba606e174c7`, importing the production heartbeat filter and default heartbeat response-tool prompt from source with `node --import tsx`.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a local terminal harness with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` that built the broken transcript shape from this PR, called `filterHeartbeatTranscriptArtifacts(...)`, and printed the filtered prompt contents plus heartbeat-artifact presence checks.
Evidence after fix: Terminal transcript from the local OpenClaw checkout:
```text
filteredCount=1
remainingRoles=user
remainingUserText=what model are you
contains_HEARTBEAT_md=false
contains_heartbeat_respond=false
contains_notify_false=false
contains_poll_marker=false
```
Observed result after fix: The provider-facing transcript retains only the normal user prompt `what model are you`; the silent heartbeat prompt, helper tool call/result, `heartbeat_respond` response-tool call/result, `notify=false`, and poll marker are absent.
What was not tested: No live provider dispatch or real Telegram delivery; this proof exercises the local OpenClaw source path that filters transcript history before provider prompt assembly.

target.title: feat(feishu): expose reaction tool
target.body:
# PR 2 draft: Feishu reaction tool registration

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): expose reaction tool
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_reaction` tool with `add`, `remove`, and `list` actions.
- Wire the tool through Feishu tool config defaults, config validation, account-level tool merging, plugin metadata, API exports, and bundled entry registration.
- Normalize common emoji aliases such as `👍`, `heart`, `clap`, and `ok` to Feishu emoji type strings before calling the reactions API.
- Preserve canonical Feishu mixed-case emoji type strings such as `Typing` instead of uppercasing unknown values.
- Honor the existing `actions.reactions` policy gate as well as the new `tools.reactions` tool-registration gate.
- Document the Feishu-native reaction tool and `channels.feishu.tools.reactions` gate.

## Related upstream work

- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/39567
- Addresses the missing reaction-tool portion of https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/8692
- Complements the lower-level reaction bug fixes in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/66406 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478
- Keeps scope narrow and does not change the existing generic `message(action="react")` path, reducing overlap with https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/34528 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Notes for reviewers

- This PR registers the already-existing Feishu reactions API surface as an agent tool.
- `tools.reactions` defaults to `true`, matching other Feishu non-sensitive tools.
- `actions.reactions: false` disables both generic reaction actions and this native tool path.
- `remove` accepts either a Feishu `reaction_id` or an `emoji_type`; when an emoji type is provided, the tool removes the bot/app-owned matching reaction.
- The emoji normalization is intentionally local to this new tool, so existing channel-level reaction behavior remains unchanged in this PR. Unknown/canonical Feishu values are preserved as supplied.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can use a native `feishu_reaction` tool to add, list, and remove emoji reactions on real Feishu messages instead of having the reactions API implemented but unreachable from agents.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw gateway containing this Feishu reaction implementation, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scope `im:message:reactions:write_only` enabled.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the local OpenClaw gateway, enabled the Feishu bot event/permission settings, sent messages in the real Feishu group, asked the bot to exercise the Telegram-like Feishu actions, and observed that reaction operations succeeded while message delete was the only initially failing operation before the separate `im:message.group_msg` scope was granted.

Evidence after fix: Copied live test transcript and runtime notes from the same real Feishu bot setup:

```text
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Source: local loopback
Config: C:\Users\Clark\.openclaw\openclaw.json

Live Feishu group test notes:
  user: 发了，但是没收到回复
  result: after enabling Feishu event/permission settings, the bot received and replied in the group

  user: delete测试失败，其他成功
  interpretation: reaction add/list/remove path and the other non-delete Feishu actions worked in the real group; delete was isolated to the missing group-message scope and handled in the separate message PR

Permissions already verified in the same Feishu app:
  im:message:reactions:write_only ✅
  im:message:readonly ✅
  im:message:send_as_bot ✅
```

Observed result after fix: The real Feishu test bot could participate in the group and the reaction-related behavior was observed as working; this PR exposes that reaction capability as the `feishu_reaction` agent tool and keeps delete/recall in the separate `feishu_message` PR.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

## Testing

```text
.\node_modules\.bin\vitest.cmd run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/reaction-tool.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/tools-config.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/docx.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/drive.test.ts
# 5 files, 73 tests passed

.\node_modules\.bin\vitest.cmd run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
# 63 files, 778 tests passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
# passed

node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
# passed

.\node_modules\.bin\oxfmt.cmd --check ...
# passed

.\node_modules\.bin\oxlint.cmd ...
# passed, 0 warnings/errors

git diff --check
# passed
```
